news_summary: Progress in human rights is marked by enhanced protection for climate-displaced individuals in Africa and a transformative, child-centered approach in family court proceedings.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès en matière de droits de l'homme se traduisent par une protection renforcée des personnes déplacées pour des raisons climatiques en Afrique et par une approche novatrice, centrée sur l'enfant, dans les procédures devant les tribunaux aux affaires familiales.
news_summary_es: Los avances en materia de derechos humanos se caracterizan por una mayor protección de las personas desplazadas por el cambio climático en África y por un enfoque transformador y centrado en el niño en los procedimientos de los tribunales de familia.

articles.title: African Court Should Protect People Displaced by Climate Change
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Two people working on a sunken boat  in the Langue de Barbarie, Saint-Louis, Senegal, March 7, 2023.
                    © 2023 Raquel Maria Carbonell Pagola/LightRocket via Getty Images
          
    


  
The African Court on Human and Peoples’ Rights is poised to issue an advisory opinion on states’ human rights obligations in the climate crisis. This is an opportunity to strengthen protections for people displaced by climate change and to call for a rights-respecting approach. A petition filed in May 2025 by the Pan African Lawyers Union and the African Climate Platform asked the court to interpret states’ obligations under the African Charter on Human and Peoples’ Rights in light of climate change. This comes amid a surge in such legal clarifications by international and regional tribunals.   



      

  
  
    



  
        
  

          
        Human Rights Watch Submission for the African Court Advisory Opinion on State Obligations Related to the Climate Crisis
      
      


  
  



      
  



            
        
                        
      
          
      
        
    
      August 18, 2025
            
        “Waiting for God”

      
      Flood Displacement and Planned Relocation of Fisherfolk in Saint-Louis, Senegal
Download the full report in English
Download the Summary & Recommendations in Wolof
Annex 1a: Human Rights Watch Letter to the World Bank
Annex 1b: World Bank Response to Human Rights Watch
Annex 2a: Human Rights Watch Letter to the Municipal Development Agency of Senegal
Annex 2b: Municipal Development Agency of Senegal Response to Human Rights Watch
Annex 3: Human Rights Watch Letter to the Ministry of the Environment of Senegal 
Annex 4: Human Rights Watch Letter to the Mayor of Saint-Louis, Senegal
Annex 5: Human Rights Watch Letter to the Prefect of Saint-Louis, Senegal
Annex 6: Human Rights Watch Letter to the Governor of Saint-Louis, Senegal
Annex 7: Human Rights Watch Letter to the Saint-Louis Municipal Development Agency 
Annex 8: Human Rights Watch Letter to the Ministry of Localities and Territorial Management of Senegal
Annex 9: Human Rights Watch Letter to the Regional Environmental Ministry of Saint-Louis 
Human Rights Watch submitted an amicus brief to the court drawing on our August 2025 report, “‘Waiting for God’: Flood Displacement and Planned Relocation of Fisherfolk in Saint-Louis, Senegal.” Coastal flooding in 2015 and 2016 displaced fishing families from the Langue de Barbarie peninsula in Saint-Louis to Khar Yalla, a site that government and World Bank officials acknowledge is unsuitable for permanent habitation. We found that nearly a decade after the floods, families experienced severe overcrowding, most houses lacked electricity, there was no waste collection, and seasonal flooding sent septic water into homes. These families are excluded from a World Bank-funded planned relocation of others displaced by coastal floods, and are desperate for the kind of protections this advisory opinion could make clear are state obligations.Based on data from the Internal Displacement Monitoring Center, the number of people internally displaced by floods and other natural hazards has risen nearly sixfold across African continent in the last 15 years, to 6.3 million in 2023. Planned relocations are already happening across Africa with 39 cases identified in a global mapping. The 2025 decisions by the International Court of Justice and the Inter-American Court of Human Rights cemented the notion that climate policy must respect human rights, be guided by the best available science, and reflect a state’s highest level of ambition. But no international tribunal has comprehensively clarified how national policies can protect the rights of climate displaced communities awaiting planned relocations.The court should address this critical gap for communities like Khar Yalla by clarifying that states parties to the African Charter have binding obligations to protect people displaced by climate change including through rights-respecting planned relocation as a last resort.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Two people working on a sunken boat in the Langue de Barbarie, Saint-Louis, Senegal, March 7, 2023. © 2023 Raquel Maria Carbonell Pagola/LightRocket via Getty Images

The African Court on Human and Peoples’ Rights is poised to issue an advisory opinion on states’ human rights obligations in the climate crisis. This is an opportunity to strengthen protections for people displaced by climate change and to call for a rights-respecting approach.

A petition filed in May 2025 by the Pan African Lawyers Union and the African Climate Platform asked the court to interpret states’ obligations under the African Charter on Human and Peoples’ Rights in light of climate change. This comes amid a surge in such legal clarifications by international and regional tribunals.

Human Rights Watch submitted an amicus brief to the court drawing on our August 2025 report, “‘Waiting for God’: Flood Displacement and Planned Relocation of Fisherfolk in Saint-Louis, Senegal.” Coastal flooding in 2015 and 2016 displaced fishing families from the Langue de Barbarie peninsula in Saint-Louis to Khar Yalla, a site that government and World Bank officials acknowledge is unsuitable for permanent habitation.

We found that nearly a decade after the floods, families experienced severe overcrowding, most houses lacked electricity, there was no waste collection, and seasonal flooding sent septic water into homes. These families are excluded from a World Bank-funded planned relocation of others displaced by coastal floods, and are desperate for the kind of protections this advisory opinion could make clear are state obligations.

Based on data from the Internal Displacement Monitoring Center, the number of people internally displaced by floods and other natural hazards has risen nearly sixfold across African continent in the last 15 years, to 6.3 million in 2023. Planned relocations are already happening across Africa with 39 cases identified in a global mapping.

The 2025 decisions by the International Court of Justice and the Inter-American Court of Human Rights cemented the notion that climate policy must respect human rights, be guided by the best available science, and reflect a state’s highest level of ambition. But no international tribunal has comprehensively clarified how national policies can protect the rights of climate displaced communities awaiting planned relocations.

The court should address this critical gap for communities like Khar Yalla by clarifying that states parties to the African Charter have binding obligations to protect people displaced by climate change including through rights-respecting planned relocation as a last resort.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant legal development where the African Court on Human and Peoples’ Rights is set to clarify states' obligations to protect people displaced by climate change, a pressing humanitarian issue affecting millions across Africa. This advisory opinion has broad implications for human rights protections in climate policy and planned relocations, addressing a critical gap with potential large-scale positive impact.
articles.personality_title: African Court to clarify states’ duties to protect climate-displaced people
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people in Africa are forced to leave their homes because of floods and other natural disasters linked to climate change. These displacements have increased a lot in recent years, but it is not always clear what governments must do to help those affected.

**What happened** – In 2025, the African Court on Human and Peoples’ Rights is set to give an advisory opinion. This opinion will explain what responsibilities African countries have under the African Charter on Human and Peoples’ Rights when dealing with people displaced by climate change. This follows a petition by legal and climate groups and includes evidence from a recent report about fishing families in Senegal who were forced to move after floods.

**Impact** – This advisory opinion is important because it could make clear that governments must protect displaced people’s rights. For example, in Senegal, families living in overcrowded and unsafe conditions after floods are not included in planned relocations funded by the World Bank. The court’s guidance could help ensure such communities receive better support and that relocations respect their rights.

**What's next step** – Once the court issues its opinion, African states will have clearer rules about their duties to protect people forced to move by climate change. This could influence how governments plan and carry out relocations in a way that respects human rights and uses the best science available.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The African Court’s upcoming opinion will help define how countries must protect the rights of millions displaced by climate change across Africa.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Cour africaine clarifie les devoirs des États envers les personnes déplacées par le climat
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses personnes en Afrique sont contraintes de quitter leur domicile en raison des inondations et d’autres catastrophes naturelles liées au changement climatique. Ces déplacements ont beaucoup augmenté ces dernières années, mais les obligations des gouvernements restent floues.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2025, la Cour africaine des droits de l’homme et des peuples doit rendre un avis consultatif. Cet avis expliquera quelles responsabilités les pays africains ont selon la Charte africaine des droits de l’homme et des peuples face aux personnes déplacées par le changement climatique. Cela fait suite à une pétition d’organisations juridiques et climatiques, avec un rapport récent sur des familles de pêcheurs au Sénégal déplacées par des inondations.

**Impact** – Cet avis est important car il pourrait clarifier que les gouvernements doivent protéger les droits des déplacés. Par exemple, au Sénégal, des familles vivent dans des conditions surpeuplées et dangereuses après les inondations, sans être incluses dans les relocalisations planifiées financées par la Banque mondiale. La décision de la Cour pourrait garantir un meilleur soutien à ces communautés et que les relocalisations respectent leurs droits.

**Prochaine étape** – Après l’avis de la Cour, les États africains auront des règles plus claires sur leurs devoirs envers les personnes déplacées par le climat. Cela influencera la façon dont les gouvernements planifient et réalisent les relocalisations en respectant les droits humains et les meilleures données scientifiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’avis prochain de la Cour africaine définira comment les pays doivent protéger les droits de millions de personnes déplacées par le changement climatique en Afrique.
articles.personality_title_es: La Corte Africana aclarará deberes estatales hacia personas desplazadas por el clima
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas en África se ven obligadas a dejar sus hogares debido a inundaciones y otros desastres naturales relacionados con el cambio climático. Estos desplazamientos han aumentado mucho en los últimos años, pero no siempre está claro qué deben hacer los gobiernos para ayudar.

**Qué pasó** – En 2025, la Corte Africana de Derechos Humanos y de los Pueblos emitirá una opinión consultiva. Esta opinión explicará las responsabilidades que tienen los países africanos bajo la Carta Africana de Derechos Humanos y de los Pueblos para proteger a las personas desplazadas por el cambio climático. Esto sigue a una petición de grupos legales y climáticos, y un informe sobre familias de pescadores en Senegal desplazadas por inundaciones.

**Impacto** – Esta opinión es importante porque puede dejar claro que los gobiernos deben proteger los derechos de las personas desplazadas. Por ejemplo, en Senegal, familias que viven en condiciones hacinadas y peligrosas tras las inundaciones no están incluidas en las reubicaciones planificadas financiadas por el Banco Mundial. La opinión de la Corte podría ayudar a que estas comunidades reciban mejor apoyo y que las reubicaciones respeten sus derechos.

**Próximo paso** – Cuando la Corte emita su opinión, los países africanos tendrán reglas más claras sobre sus deberes para proteger a las personas desplazadas por el cambio climático. Esto podría influir en cómo planifican y realizan las reubicaciones respetando los derechos humanos y la mejor ciencia disponible.

**Una frase resumen** – La próxima opinión de la Corte Africana definirá cómo los países deben proteger los derechos de millones desplazados por el cambio climático en África.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a gently rising shoreline where a small, weathered fishing boat rests partly submerged near a narrow peninsula, with two silhouetted figures working together on it, surrounded by calm waters reflecting soft natural tones, and in the background, simple humble homes clustered closely on the land, symbolizing resilience and community amid gradual coastal change.

articles.title: ‘Child-focused’ approach in family court battles is biggest change in 30 years, top judge says
articles.summary: Sir Andrew McFarlane welcomes the government backing for the change, saying it will turn the old approach "on its head".
articles.content: The BBC is following a case in which parents have been fighting over a six-year-old girl for almost her entire life. It is only within the last year or so that the little girl's voice - her wishes and feelings - have been reported to the court, through a social worker from the independent Children and Court Advisory Service (Cafcass).
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant reform in family court proceedings that prioritizes the child's voice and welfare, representing a meaningful change in the justice system with broad societal impact. This child-focused approach improves the protection and consideration of children's rights in legal disputes, which is an inspiring and substantial development.
articles.personality_title: New family court rules put children’s voices first after 30 years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Family courts have long handled disputes between parents, often focusing on legal arguments rather than what children want or need. For decades, children’s opinions were rarely heard in these cases.

**What happened** – Recently, the UK government supported a big change in family court rules. Now, courts must listen to children’s wishes and feelings, often with help from social workers from the Children and Court Advisory Service (Cafcass). For example, a six-year-old girl’s voice was finally shared in court after years of her parents’ ongoing fight.

**Impact** – This new approach is important because it focuses on the child’s well-being instead of just the parents’ disputes. It helps protect children’s rights and makes sure their feelings matter in decisions about their lives. Judges say this is the biggest change in 30 years and will change how family courts work.

**What’s next step** – Courts will continue using this child-focused method in more cases. Officials will watch how well it works and may make more improvements to better support children in family disputes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Family courts are now putting children’s voices at the center of decisions, marking a major improvement in how family disputes are handled.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les tribunaux familiaux placent enfin la voix des enfants au cœur des décisions
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les tribunaux familiaux traitent depuis longtemps les conflits entre parents, en se concentrant souvent sur les arguments juridiques plutôt que sur les souhaits des enfants. Pendant des décennies, les avis des enfants étaient rarement pris en compte.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Récemment, le gouvernement britannique a soutenu un grand changement dans les règles des tribunaux familiaux. Désormais, les tribunaux doivent écouter les souhaits et les sentiments des enfants, souvent avec l’aide des travailleurs sociaux du service d’aide aux enfants et aux tribunaux (Cafcass). Par exemple, la voix d’une fillette de six ans a enfin été entendue après des années de disputes entre ses parents.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle méthode est importante car elle met le bien-être de l’enfant au centre, au lieu de se concentrer uniquement sur les conflits des parents. Elle protège les droits des enfants et garantit que leurs sentiments comptent dans les décisions concernant leur vie. Les juges disent que c’est le plus grand changement depuis 30 ans et que cela va transformer le fonctionnement des tribunaux familiaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les tribunaux continueront à appliquer cette méthode centrée sur l’enfant dans plus de cas. Les autorités surveilleront son efficacité et pourront apporter d’autres améliorations pour mieux soutenir les enfants dans les conflits familiaux.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les tribunaux familiaux placent désormais la voix des enfants au centre des décisions, marquant une avancée majeure dans la gestion des conflits familiaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Los tribunales de familia ponen la voz de los niños en primer lugar tras 30 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los tribunales de familia han resuelto disputas entre padres centrándose en argumentos legales y no en lo que los niños desean o necesitan. Durante décadas, la opinión de los niños rara vez se escuchaba.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, el gobierno del Reino Unido apoyó un gran cambio en las normas de los tribunales de familia. Ahora, los tribunales deben escuchar los deseos y sentimientos de los niños, con la ayuda de trabajadores sociales del Servicio Asesor para Niños y Tribunales (Cafcass). Por ejemplo, la voz de una niña de seis años se presentó en el tribunal después de años de pelea entre sus padres.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo enfoque es importante porque pone el bienestar del niño en el centro, en lugar de solo los conflictos de los padres. Ayuda a proteger los derechos de los niños y asegura que sus sentimientos importen en las decisiones sobre sus vidas. Los jueces dicen que es el cambio más grande en 30 años y que transformará el trabajo de los tribunales de familia.

**Próximos pasos** – Los tribunales seguirán usando este método centrado en el niño en más casos. Las autoridades observarán cómo funciona y podrían hacer más mejoras para apoyar mejor a los niños en disputas familiares.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los tribunales de familia ahora ponen la voz de los niños en el centro de las decisiones, marcando una mejora importante en el manejo de conflictos familiares.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle, detailed painting of a small, brightly glowing lantern held carefully by two larger, protective hands on either side, set against a soft, neutral background, symbolizing a child's voice being nurtured and heard within a caring family court environment.
